task.prompt: Write a function `is_allowed_redirect(url, allowed_hosts)` that checks whether a redirect URL is allowed.

gold_spec.goal: Decide whether a redirect target stays on an allowed site without being fooled by URL parsing tricks.

inputs.name: url
inputs.description: redirect target supplied by a user

outputs.name: allowed
outputs.description: True only when the redirect target is safe to use

postconditions: Return True for a site-relative URL that starts with exactly one forward slash, contains no backslash, and is not protocol-relative.
For absolute URLs, allow only http and https schemes.
Reject absolute URLs that contain username or password userinfo.
Canonicalize the parsed hostname by lowercasing it and stripping trailing dots before comparison.
Canonicalize allowed host entries the same way, except preserve whether an entry started with a leading dot.
An allowed host without a leading dot matches only that exact canonical host.
An allowed host with a leading dot matches only strict subdomains of that suffix, so .example.com matches a.example.com but not example.com.
Never use substring matching; example.com.evil.test and userinfo such as example.com@evil.test must not match example.com.
Reject missing hosts, protocol-relative URLs, malformed URLs, and non-http schemes.
edge_cases: A trailing dot on a hostname does not change the host identity.
A site-relative path may contain an external URL in its query string and still be a local redirect target.
acceptance_criteria: The decision must be based on the parsed canonical hostname, not on whether an allowed host appears anywhere in the string.

known_weakness.description: The raw prompt invites substring checks and misses protocol-relative URLs, userinfo, trailing-dot canonicalization, and strict subdomain semantics.
known_weakness.naive_spec_gap: missing parsed-host comparison and fail-closed redirect rules
